name: mimalloc
summary: Daan Leijen's general-purpose allocator from Microsoft Research using page-local sharded free lists with three lists per page (`free`, `local_free`, `thread_free`) that enforce a temporal cadence between fast-path allocation and batched maintenance.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Daan Leijen's mimalloc (Microsoft Research) was originally designed as the runtime allocator for Lean and Koka — languages whose Perceus reference counting hammers the allocator with short-lived objects. The headline idea is page-local sharded free lists: every page carries three lists (`free`, `local_free`, `thread_free`) that enforce a *temporal cadence* between fast-path allocation and batched maintenance, so the alloc/free fast path stays branchless while deferred-free and remote-free are merged in lock-free.

The library is small, portable C, and ships as a drop-in `LD_PRELOAD`-style replacement for the system `malloc`. It is the canonical "throughput-oriented modern allocator" alongside snmalloc and rpmalloc.

sections.title: Three-list sharding and secure mode
sections.content:
Each page owns three free lists: `free` (consumed by the fast path on the owning thread), `local_free` (where the owning thread parks frees during a quantum), and `thread_free` (a lock-free MPSC list where remote threads push frees). Periodically the owner swaps `free <- local_free` and merges `thread_free` in. This gives a branchless allocation fast path while still amortising the cost of cross-thread free.

Free-list pointers are encoded with per-page random keys: a corruption flips the encoding and detection happens on next pop. Secure mode adds guard pages around metadata, randomized initialization, and double-free detection at roughly 10% overhead. A separate "guarded mode" places sampled OS guard pages (e.g. one in 4000 allocations) so production builds catch UBSan-style overflow without paying for guard pages on every allocation.

sections.title: Deployment
sections.content:
Production users include the Lean and Koka runtimes (its design driver), Redis, .NET runtime experiments, and the Swift/Python reference-counting backends — all workloads where reference counting or short-lived object churn dominates. The Perceus algorithm in Koka was tuned alongside mimalloc; together they hit numbers competitive with tracing GCs on benchmarks where allocators are the bottleneck.

Status (as of 2026-04): widely deployed; a stable v2 series with an in-progress v3 generation maintained by Microsoft Research.
